variables.content: In finite periodic simulation cells using Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions (where the Jastrow and backflow factors each contain variational parameters), reoptimizing the Jastrow and backflow parameters for an excited-state configuration (an excitation produced by adding or removing an electron in a particular Bloch state) can lower the total DMC energy in that finite cell—for example, removing an electron from 𝗸=0 in a 26-electron paramagnetic 2D HEG at density parameter r_s=1 reduced the DMC total energy by 0.000854(4) hartree atomic units (a.u.)—but this finite-cell energy lowering is a manifestation of finite-size error and tends to reduce the computed excitation bandwidth and thus amplify finite-size bias in bandwidth-derived quantities such as the quasiparticle effective mass; therefore, to avoid introducing extra finite-size bias the Jastrow and backflow parameters are optimized in the ground state and then reused unchanged for excited-state energy evaluations.

Reoptimization of the explicit correlation parameters in finite-cell excited-state trial wave functions produces a measurable lowering of the DMC total energy of that finite cell and thereby increases the finite-size bias of excitation energies computed as total-energy differences. Concretely, when the Jastrow-factor and backflow-function parameters were reoptimized after removing an electron from wavevector \\(\\mathbf{k}=0\\) in a paramagnetic 26-electron 2D HEG at density parameter \\(r_{s}=1\\), the DMC total energy was lowered by 0.000854(4) atomic units (a.u.), which reduced the finite-cell bandwidth and therefore increased the finite-size error in the derived excitation energies. Consequently, to avoid introducing this additional finite-cell bias into excitation-energy estimates, the Jastrow and backflow parameters were optimized once in the closed-shell ground state for each density and system size and then held fixed for all corresponding excited-state DMC calculations.

Let a Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave function for a finite periodic simulation cell be parameterized by Jastrow and backflow parameters that were optimized by variational Monte Carlo (VMC) for the closed-shell ground state; denote these ground-state-optimized parameter vectors by \\((J_{\\mathrm{GS}},B_{\\mathrm{GS}})\\). The proposition is: when computing excitation energies in that finite simulation cell (energy differences between excited and ground states obtained by DMC), using the same fixed parameter values \\((J_{\\mathrm{GS}},B_{\\mathrm{GS}})\\) for the excited-state trial wave functions introduces less additional finite-size bias into the excitation energies than performing an independent reoptimization of \\(J,B\\) in each excited-state finite cell, because excited-state reoptimization in a finite cell can exploit finite-size-dependent changes in correlation that do not persist in the thermodynamic limit.

Let a Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave function for an $N$-electron ground state be $\Psi_0(\mathbf{R};\{p\})$, where $\mathbf{R}$ denotes all electronic coordinates and $\{p\}$ denotes the set of Jastrow and backflow parameters optimized in the ground state; construct $(N\pm1)$-electron trial wave functions $\Psi_{\pm}(\mathbf{R},\mathbf{r}_{\mathrm{extra}};\{p\})$ by extending the same ground-state Jastrow and backflow functions to include the extra electron in a plane-wave orbital $\exp(i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{r})$ without reoptimizing the parameters $\{p\}$ for the $(N\pm1)$ system.  The proposition is that, for the system sizes, densities, and wave-function parameterizations used in the reported calculations, this omission of excited-state reoptimization introduces only a negligible bias in the DMC energy differences $E_{\pm}^{\mathrm{DMC}}-E_0^{\mathrm{DMC}}$ compared with the statistical errors and the other systematic uncertainties claimed.

Let $E_{\mathrm{VMC}}(\Psi_{\mathrm{T}})$ denote the variational Monte Carlo expectation value of the Hamiltonian for a Slater-Jastrow trial wave function $\Psi_{\mathrm{T}}$ and let $E_{\mathrm{DMC}}(\Psi_{\mathrm{T}})$ denote the fixed-node DMC total energy obtained using the nodal surface specified by the determinantal part of $\Psi_{\mathrm{T}}$. For the molecular examples studied, reoptimizing the single-particle orbitals $\{\phi_i\}$ of $\Psi_{\mathrm{T}}$ in the presence of the Jastrow factor using the state-average EFP method lowers $E_{\mathrm{VMC}}$ by roughly $10$–$15\ \mathrm{mHartree}$ per state (ground or excited), while the corresponding changes in $E_{\mathrm{DMC}}$ are typically only a few mHartree, so that orbital reoptimization produces substantial variational improvements at the VMC level but only modest improvements in fixed-node DMC total energies; therefore, reductions in VMC energy are not a one-to-one proxy for reductions in fixed-node error.

In fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculations that employ a trial wave function \Psi_{\mathrm{T}}=\mathcal{J}\Phi with possibly nonlocal pseudopotentials handled by localization on \Psi_{\mathrm{T}} (i.e. the pseudopotential operator is evaluated using the trial function to produce a local potential), the fixed-node DMC excitation energy of an excited-state determinant \Phi_{\mathrm{exc}} relative to a ground-state determinant \Phi_{\mathrm{gr}} is primarily determined by the quality of the determinantal nodal surfaces of \Phi_{\mathrm{exc}} and \Phi_{\mathrm{gr}}; improving \Phi_{\mathrm{exc}} and/or \Phi_{\mathrm{gr}} by EFP orbital-plus-CI optimization in the presence of the Jastrow tends to lower the fixed-node excitation energy toward the exact value. However, the fixed-node DMC energy for an excited-state trial function is not variational except when the state is the lowest state in a one-dimensional irreducible representation of the molecular point group, and the computed excitation energy remains sensitive to finite-basis-set effects, the pseudopotential localization approximation, and residual nodal errors that are not removed by the variational optimization of the determinantal component.

Comparing two trial-wave-function protocols for quasiparticle-band calculations of paramagnetic 2D-UEL—(A) using a single trial wave function optimized for the ground state and applied to all k vectors, and (B) reoptimizing the trial wave function by energy minimization separately for each excited-state k-point—shows that reoptimization (B) slightly increases the VMC estimate of the quasiparticle effective mass m* relative to protocol (A), whereas the corresponding impact on fixed-node DMC m* is negligible within statistical uncertainty for the tested cases (for example N = 146 and r_s = 1, 2, 5).

Reoptimizing the determinantal component of a Slater-Jastrow trial wave function — specifically both the single-particle molecular orbitals $\{\phi_i\}$ entering the determinants and the linear configuration-interaction coefficients $\{d_n\}$ in the expansion $D(\mathbf{R})=\sum_n d_n D_n[\{\phi_i\}](\mathbf{R})$ — within variational Monte Carlo using a state-average energy-fluctuation-potential (EFP) procedure yields systematically improved trial functions for excited-state fixed-node DMC calculations. In the studied molecular examples, orbital reoptimization in the presence of the positive Jastrow factor lowers the variational Monte Carlo (VMC) energy by approximately $10$–$15\ \mathrm{mHartree}$ per state (ground or excited) but produces only modest changes of a few mHartree in the fixed-node DMC total energies, so that large VMC energy gains do not necessarily imply equally large reductions of fixed-node error. Reoptimizing the linear CI coefficients $\{d_n\}$ in the presence of the Jastrow factor produces more compact determinantal expansions (for example, fewer determinants above a coefficient threshold $\tau=0.01$) and yields lower VMC and fixed-node DMC energies compared to using the original state-average CASSCF coefficients truncated at the same threshold; these energy improvements are consistent with an improved nodal surface for the excited-state DMC calculation when coefficients are reoptimized with EFP.

Let a sequence of trial wave functions $\{ \Psi^{(n)} \}$ be constructed by increasing the number or flexibility of variational parameters (e.g., enlarging the determinantal expansion and reoptimizing all Jastrow, CSF and orbital parameters in VMC). Define $E_{\mathrm{DMC}}[\Psi]$ as the fixed-node DMC energy computed with trial function $\Psi$. The proposition is: for the C$_2$ molecule and similar systems tested in these calculations, the sequence $E_{\mathrm{DMC}}[\Psi^{(n)}]$ decreases monotonically with $n$ as more parameters are optimized (i.e., the DMC energy improves), indicating systematic reduction of the fixed-node error under the employed optimization protocol; this monotonicity is an empirical observation for the tested systems rather than a formal theorem.

Diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) simulations reported energies for various trial wave functions at finite projection time and finite time step; for bosonic systems there is no fermionic sign constraint and, in principle, DMC projection converges to the exact bosonic ground-state energy regardless of the trial wave function in the limits of infinite projection time, zero time step, and infinite walker population. Therefore, the observation that the lowest reported DMC energy at $d=200$ is obtained with a trial wave function that includes a radial shell term is most consistently interpreted as an indication that the shell-structured trial wave function improves importance-sampling efficiency (reduces finite-projection and finite-time-step bias and variance) in these practical calculations, rather than as evidence that the shell trial state provides superior "nodal guidance" (a concept meaningful for fermionic fixed-node DMC but not directly applicable to bosons).
